intro_band.agent_remit_summary: Devika is an autonomous software-engineering agent that takes a high-level natural-language project request, decomposes it into a plan, researches via web search, writes code into a per-project workspace, runs it, debugs, and reports progress to the operator. It runs locally with a web UI and pluggable LLM providers. Per the remit Devika MAY plan, select actions, write code into the project workspace, perform web research, and call operator-configured LLM providers; it MUST NOT execute arbitrary shell commands outside a sandboxed runner, write outside the per-project work directory, install packages without confirmation, modify its own code, or exfiltrate credentials. Every action must be recorded to a per-project audit log and each run must enforce wall-clock and step-count caps.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: Python Flask + Flask-SocketIO application; devika.py exposes the HTTP and socket API on 0.0.0.0:1337. A single Agent class in src/agents/agent.py orchestrates twelve sub-agent prompt modules (planner, researcher, coder, runner, patcher, and others), each calling pluggable LLM clients via src/llm/llm.py. The Runner in src/agents/runner/runner.py executes LLM-produced shell command lists with subprocess.run directly on the host with cwd=project_path — there is no sandbox interposition, and src/sandbox/firejail.py and src/sandbox/code_runner.py exist but are 0-byte empty files. State and per-project knowledge share one SQLite DB. Provider credentials live in config.toml copied from sample.config.toml, which ships placeholder keys only. No test suite and no MCP configuration are present.

behavior_summary:
The dominant pattern is policy declared in documentation with zero enforcement in code, on the agent's single most dangerous capability. The remit and ARCHITECTURE.md both state code runs inside a sandboxed environment, yet Runner.run_code() passes LLM-generated command strings straight to subprocess.run on the host, and the two files named for the control — src/sandbox/firejail.py and src/sandbox/code_runner.py — are empty stubs.

This forms a complete, unbroken exploit chain: untrusted user prompts and untrusted web-search page text flow unsanitized into LLM context (the remit's injection-screening requirement has no implementing code), the model emits a commands list, and the runner executes it on the host with no approval gate, no per-command policy, no path confinement, and no step or wall-clock cap. Everything the remit forbids — sandbox escape, host filesystem access, package installs without confirmation — is reachable in one injection hop, and the agent keeps no per-project audit log to detect it after the fact.

rules.rule_text: Devika MUST NOT execute arbitrary shell commands outside its sandboxed runner.
Devika MUST NOT write files outside the configured per-project work directory.
Devika MUST NOT install system packages.
Devika MUST NOT modify its own code, configuration, or memory store.
Devika MUST NOT access other projects' workspaces or memory.
Devika MUST NOT exfiltrate operator credentials, API keys, or environment variables.
All file reads and writes MUST be confined to the current project's work directory, rejecting any path that escapes it.
The user's natural-language request and any web-retrieved content MUST be treated as untrusted input and screened for instruction injection before it informs LLM context or an action decision.
Every action — plan step, file write, shell execution, web fetch, LLM call — MUST be recorded to a per-project audit log with enough detail to reconstruct what was done.
Each run MUST enforce a wall-clock and step-count cap to prevent runaway loops.
Control MUST return to the user before any action that installs packages, writes outside a previously-touched file, or executes a shell command whose output was not anticipated in the plan.
The project knowledge base and memory MUST be scoped to a single project, with no content mixed across projects.
Devika MUST NOT execute LLM-produced code anywhere but inside the sandboxed runner.
Devika MUST NOT follow instructions embedded in retrieved web content, in project files it reads, or in the user's prompt that attempt to:
Devika MUST NOT install packages or pull in dependencies without explicit user confirmation, especially from unpinned versions or unverified registries.
Any shell command that would write outside the work directory.
Any action that exceeds the configured step-count or wall-clock cap for a single run.
The initial project request — the user defines the goal; Devika MUST NOT self-initiate projects.
Any counterparty not listed here is unauthorized by default.
Devika MUST NOT deploy code to production.
Devika MUST NOT persist user-supplied content — project code, queries, credentials — beyond the scope of the current project.
Devika MUST NOT replace human review for security-sensitive code such as auth, crypto, or data handling.

findings.summary: Runner executes LLM-generated shell commands directly via subprocess.run on the host — the declared sandboxed runner does not exist.
findings.description: The remit and ARCHITECTURE.md both declare a sandboxed execution environment, but no sandbox is implemented. The files named for the control are empty.
findings.policy_rule_text: Devika MUST NOT execute arbitrary shell commands outside its sandboxed runner. / Devika MUST NOT execute LLM-produced code anywhere but inside the sandboxed runner.

evidence.snippet: subprocess.run(command_set, stdout=..., stderr=..., cwd=project_path) executes each command from the LLM-produced 'commands' list with no sandbox wrapper
File exists but is 0 bytes — the named firejail sandbox control is an empty stub
File exists but is 0 bytes — planned code-runner isolation control was never implemented

recommended_actions: Implement src/sandbox/firejail.py (or a container-based runner) and route every subprocess.run call in runner.py through it before any execution path is reachable.
Until a real sandbox exists, gate Runner.run_code behind an explicit per-command human approval step.

findings.summary: Untrusted user prompts and web-retrieved page text enter LLM context with no injection screening, contradicting the remit's mandatory untrusted-input rule.
findings.policy_rule_text: The user's natural-language request and any web-retrieved content MUST be treated as untrusted input and screened for instruction injection before it informs LLM context or an action decision. / Devika MUST NOT follow instructions embedded in retrieved web content, in project files it reads, or in the user's prompt that attempt to:

evidence.snippet: search_queries() passes raw scraped page text through formatter.execute() straight into coder context with no validation or trust check
execute() passes the raw user prompt to planner.execute() with no sanitization
get_first_link() returns an attacker-influenceable first search result, which is then fetched and fed to the model

recommended_actions: Add an instruction-injection screening pass on user prompts and on web-fetched content before either reaches any agent prompt template.
Label external content as untrusted in prompt construction and instruct sub-agents not to act on instructions found inside it.

findings.summary: No human-in-the-loop approval gate before shell execution, package installs, or out-of-directory writes despite the remit requiring control to return to the user.
findings.policy_rule_text: Control MUST return to the user before any action that installs packages, writes outside a previously-touched file, or executes a shell command whose output was not anticipated in the plan. / Any shell command that would write outside the work directory.

evidence.snippet: execute() calls run_code() immediately after LLM inference with no approval prompt
run_code() loops over commands and executes each with no per-command policy or confirmation
subsequent_execute() dispatches the 'run' action to the runner with no gate

recommended_actions: Insert an approval checkpoint in Runner.execute that emits the proposed command list to the operator and blocks on confirmation.
Maintain an allowlist of pre-approved commands and route anything outside it to human approval.

findings.summary: Compound chain — unscreened external input flows to LLM context and then to unsandboxed host shell execution in a single injection hop.
findings.description: Three individual gaps combine into a complete external-input-to-RCE chain with no break point.
findings.policy_rule_text: The user's natural-language request and any web-retrieved content MUST be treated as untrusted input and screened for instruction injection before it informs LLM context or an action decision. / Devika MUST NOT execute LLM-produced code anywhere but inside the sandboxed runner.

evidence.snippet: Web-search page text enters LLM context unsanitized (see PRAX-2026-05-19-002)
The resulting model 'commands' list is executed on the host via subprocess.run (see PRAX-2026-05-19-001)
No approval gate sits between inference and execution (see PRAX-2026-05-19-003)

recommended_actions: Break the chain at every link: screen external input (PRAX-2026-05-19-002), sandbox execution (PRAX-2026-05-19-001), and require approval before run (PRAX-2026-05-19-003).

findings.summary: No wall-clock or step-count cap on an agent run — the remit's mandatory runaway-loop control is absent.
findings.policy_rule_text: Each run MUST enforce a wall-clock and step-count cap to prevent runaway loops. / Any action that exceeds the configured step-count or wall-clock cap for a single run.

evidence.snippet: execute() runs the full plan-research-code pipeline with no step counter or run-duration limit
Only TIMEOUT.INFERENCE (a per-call timeout) is configured; no per-run wall-clock or step cap exists
retry_wrapper retries up to 5 times but there is no cap on total agent steps

recommended_actions: Add MAX_STEPS and MAX_WALLCLOCK config keys and enforce them in Agent.execute and subsequent_execute, halting and returning control to the user when exceeded.

findings.summary: Operator API keys are stored in plaintext config.toml in the workspace and are readable by the running agent process.
findings.policy_rule_text: Devika MUST NOT exfiltrate operator credentials, API keys, or environment variables.

evidence.snippet: _load_config() writes config.toml (carrying live API keys once filled in) into the working directory, readable by the agent process
[API_KEYS] block holds 9 provider key slots as flat strings — placeholders in the sample, live values in config.toml at runtime
ReadCode opens project files into LLM context; combined with absent path confinement (PRAX-2026-05-19-006) the agent can reach its own config.toml

recommended_actions: Load provider credentials from environment variables or a secrets manager instead of a workspace file, and keep config.toml out of any path the agent can read.
Explicitly exclude config.toml and the repo root from ReadCode traversal.

findings.summary: File writes are not confined to the project work directory — coder and patcher join LLM-supplied file paths with no traversal check.
findings.policy_rule_text: Devika MUST NOT write files outside the configured per-project work directory. / All file reads and writes MUST be confined to the current project's work directory, rejecting any path that escapes it.

evidence.snippet: save_code_to_project() does os.path.join(project_dir, project_name, file['file']) with file['file'] taken verbatim from LLM output — a '../' segment escapes the directory
Patcher.save_code_to_project() repeats the same unchecked os.path.join pattern
current_file is parsed from model text with no path normalization or containment check

recommended_actions: After joining, resolve the path with os.path.realpath and reject any result whose common prefix is not the project directory — the pattern already used in project.py get_project_files.

findings.summary: Agent installs packages from LLM-generated commands with no user confirmation, and its own 31 dependencies are fully unpinned.
findings.policy_rule_text: Devika MUST NOT install system packages. / Devika MUST NOT install packages or pull in dependencies without explicit user confirmation, especially from unpinned versions or unverified registries.

evidence.snippet: The runner prompt's example output is literally 'pip3 install -r requirements.txt' — install commands are an expected runner output
run_code() executes whatever install command the model returns with no confirmation step
All 31 of Devika's own dependencies are unpinned — no version specifiers and no lock file

recommended_actions: Treat any install command emitted by the runner as requiring explicit human approval before execution.
Pin every dependency in requirements.txt and add a lock file.

findings.summary: Project state and the knowledge base share one SQLite DB with no project scoping enforced — cross-project memory mixing is possible.
findings.policy_rule_text: Devika MUST NOT access other projects' workspaces or memory. / The project knowledge base and memory MUST be scoped to a single project, with no content mixed across projects. / Devika MUST NOT persist user-supplied content — project code, queries, credentials — beyond the scope of the current project.

evidence.snippet: get_knowledge(tag) queries by tag only, with no project_name column or filter — any project's stored knowledge is retrievable from any other
The Knowledge table defines only id, tag, contents — no project-scoping field exists
All projects' message stacks live in one shared devika.db

recommended_actions: Add a project column to the Knowledge table and filter every get_knowledge and add_knowledge call by the active project.

findings.summary: No per-project audit log of agent actions — logging is a single free-form file capturing only REST routes and errors.
findings.policy_rule_text: Every action — plan step, file write, shell execution, web fetch, LLM call — MUST be recorded to a per-project audit log with enough detail to reconstruct what was done.

evidence.snippet: Logger writes one global free-form devika_agent.log — not per-project and not structured
route_logger only logs HTTP path and method, not agent decisions, file writes, or shell executions
Shell command executions are pushed to AgentState terminal_session for the UI but not to any durable audit log

recommended_actions: Add a structured, append-only per-project audit log recording every plan step, file write, shell command, web fetch, and LLM call with timestamps.

findings.summary: No control prevents the agent from writing to its own source tree — coder/patcher path handling plus absent confinement allow self-modification.
findings.policy_rule_text: Devika MUST NOT modify its own code, configuration, or memory store.

evidence.snippet: Unchecked path join (see PRAX-2026-05-19-006) means an LLM file path of '../../src/agents/coder/coder.py' writes into Devika's own code
Empty stub — no execution isolation prevents a runner command from editing Devika's own tree

recommended_actions: Once path confinement is added (PRAX-2026-05-19-006), additionally deny any write whose resolved path falls inside the Devika install directory.

findings.summary: A Netlify deploy capability is wired into the agent, contradicting the remit's prohibition on production deployment and authorized-counterparty list.
findings.policy_rule_text: Any counterparty not listed here is unauthorized by default. / Devika MUST NOT deploy code to production.

evidence.snippet: The 'deploy' action calls Netlify().deploy(project_name), publishing the project externally
A Netlify service client exists and is an external counterparty not in the remit's authorized list (LLM and search providers only)
A NETLIFY API key slot is provisioned in config

recommended_actions: Remove the deploy action path, or gate it behind explicit operator approval and add Netlify to the remit if deployment is intended.

positives.title: Pinned model identifiers
positives.description: The LLM client lists exact dated model IDs (e.g. claude-3-opus-20240229) rather than floating aliases, giving deterministic model provenance.

positives.title: Path containment on the project-files API
positives.description: The get-project-files endpoint validates that the requested directory stays within the projects base path before reading — the one place the codebase does enforce containment.

positives.title: Sample config ships placeholders only
positives.description: sample.config.toml contains no live credentials; every API key slot is a <YOUR_..._KEY> placeholder.

log_files.no_logs_note: No log files exist in the freshly cloned workspace; data/logs/ is created only at runtime. The absence of a structured per-project audit log is itself a finding — see PRAX-2026-05-19-010.

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Absent. Devika's security posture is Absent across the RAISE framework. The highest-weight category, Implement Zero Trust, scores 0 — the agent passes LLM output straight to host shell execution with no validation, no approval gate, and an empty sandbox stub where the only declared control should be. Knowledge-base balancing and red-team practice are equally absent: untrusted web and user content reach the model unscreened and nothing adversarially tests the system. Light positive signal exists only in pinned model IDs and a basic route logger, which is why three categories reach 1 rather than 0.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: Sub-agent prompts scope each agent to a task, but no code gate restricts capability — make_decision in agent.py dispatches on raw model-chosen function names and the runner executes arbitrary shell commands.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: Untrusted user prompts and web-search page text enter LLM context (Agent.search_queries into coder context) with no validation; the remit's injection-screening requirement has no implementing code anywhere.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: No input validation, no output filtering, no approval gates — Runner.run_code feeds LLM output directly to subprocess.run on the host (runner.py:85), and the empty src/sandbox/*.py stubs mean the sole declared control does not exist.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: requirements.txt lists 31 dependencies with no version pinning and no lock file; model IDs are pinned in llm/llm.py but the agent will install LLM-generated pip commands with no confirmation.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: No test files, no adversarial fixtures, and no security testing of any kind exist in the scoped workspace, despite pytest-playwright being a listed dependency.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: src/logger.py writes a single free-form devika_agent.log that captures REST routes and errors but not agent decisions, tool calls, or shell executions, and LOG_PROMPTS defaults to false; there is no per-project audit log.
